meta.title: SF-SPAA-2: Access Control, RBAC and Privilege Management

questions.stem: A new Snowflake account is provisioned. Which role is automatically granted to every user created in the account, including service accounts?

options.text: SYSADMIN
options.explanation: is incorrect. SYSADMIN is a system role for creating and managing database objects; it must be explicitly granted to users — it is never automatically assigned.

options.text: USERADMIN
options.explanation: is incorrect. USERADMIN is a system role for creating users and roles; it must be explicitly granted and is not assigned to users automatically.

options.text: PUBLIC
options.explanation: is correct. The PUBLIC role is automatically granted to every user in a Snowflake account upon creation, including service accounts. It represents the minimum common access level. Any privileges granted to PUBLIC are available to all users without additional grants.

options.text: SECURITYADMIN
options.explanation: is incorrect. SECURITYADMIN manages access control (MANAGE GRANTS privilege); it must be explicitly granted and is not automatically assigned to any user.

questions.stem: An administrator needs to enforce that all new users must change their password on first login. Which parameter in the CREATE USER statement controls this?

options.text: FORCE_PASSWORD_RESET = TRUE
options.explanation: is incorrect. FORCE_PASSWORD_RESET is not a valid Snowflake user parameter; the correct parameter name is MUST_CHANGE_PASSWORD.

options.text: MUST_CHANGE_PASSWORD = TRUE
options.explanation: is correct. MUST_CHANGE_PASSWORD = TRUE is a valid CREATE USER or ALTER USER parameter that forces the user to change their password at the next login. This is the standard Snowflake mechanism for enforcing first-login password changes.

options.text: PASSWORD_EXPIRY = IMMEDIATE
options.explanation: is incorrect. PASSWORD_EXPIRY = IMMEDIATE is not valid Snowflake syntax; there is no such parameter.

options.text: CHANGE_ON_LOGIN = TRUE
options.explanation: is incorrect. CHANGE_ON_LOGIN is not a valid Snowflake user parameter.

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator is designing a role hierarchy for a healthcare organization. Select TWO best practices that should be followed when designing this hierarchy. (Select TWO)

options.text: Grant custom roles to SYSADMIN so SYSADMIN can manage objects owned by those roles
options.explanation: is correct. Granting custom roles to SYSADMIN ensures SYSADMIN can manage all objects through the inherited role hierarchy — critical for centralized object administration. Without this, SYSADMIN cannot manage objects owned by custom roles.

options.text: Use ACCOUNTADMIN as the daily operational role for all senior DBAs to ensure maximum privileges
options.explanation: is incorrect. Using ACCOUNTADMIN for daily operations violates the principle of least privilege. ACCOUNTADMIN should be reserved for critical account-level operations only.

options.text: Create separate functional roles (e.g., ANALYST, ENGINEER) and access roles (e.g., READ_DB1, WRITE_DB1), granting access roles to functional roles
options.explanation: is correct. Separating functional roles (e.g., ANALYST, ENGINEER) from access roles (e.g., READ_DB1, WRITE_DB1) and granting access roles to functional roles is a documented Snowflake best practice. It makes auditing and privilege management cleaner and more scalable.

options.text: Grant all custom roles directly to PUBLIC to ensure all users have access to common resources
options.explanation: is incorrect. Granting all custom roles to PUBLIC gives every user in the account those roles' privileges — a severe security violation.

questions.stem: A Snowflake account administrator sets the following at the account level: ALTER ACCOUNT SET DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 14. A developer then creates a table with CREATE TABLE orders (...). What is the Time Travel retention period for the orders table?

options.text: 0 days, because new tables default to no retention
options.explanation: is incorrect. New tables do not default to 0 days retention; they inherit the nearest explicit setting in the hierarchy (account, database, or schema), which is 14 days here.

options.text: 1 day, which is the Snowflake default for Standard edition
options.explanation: is incorrect. 1 day is the Snowflake default for Standard edition accounts with no explicit configuration, but the account here has been set to 14 days.

options.text: 14 days, because the table inherits the account-level setting
options.explanation: is correct. When DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS is set at the account level and no explicit value is provided at the database, schema, or table level, the object inherits the account-level setting. The table was created without specifying retention, so it inherits the account setting of 14 days. Note that retention beyond 1 day requires Enterprise edition or higher.

options.text: 90 days, which is the maximum allowed retention period
options.explanation: is incorrect. 90 days is the maximum retention for Enterprise edition, but the account is explicitly set to 14 days, so the table inherits 14, not 90.

questions.stem: A company is implementing Snowflake in a regulated environment and needs to ensure that the SECURITYADMIN role cannot be used to access business data, only to manage security. Which architectural control achieves this?

options.text: Remove the SECURITYADMIN role from all users and only assign it when needed via a PAM tool
options.explanation: is incorrect. Removing SECURITYADMIN from users and using a PAM tool is an operational control, not an architectural role design. The question asks for the architectural control.

options.text: Use a network policy to prevent SECURITYADMIN users from accessing data tables
options.explanation: is correct. SECURITYADMIN has the MANAGE GRANTS global privilege but does not inherently have data access. The risk is that SECURITYADMIN can grant itself data access. The architectural control is to ensure no data privileges are granted to SECURITYADMIN and it is not placed in a role hierarchy that includes data-owning roles. This is a standard compliance control for separation of duties.

options.text: Set STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS = 0 for SECURITYADMIN to prevent data queries
options.explanation: is incorrect. Network policies restrict access by IP address, not by role. A network policy cannot prevent a role from querying data tables.

options.text: Ensure that no data access roles (SELECT, INSERT, etc.) are granted to SECURITYADMIN, and that SECURITYADMIN is not granted SYSADMIN or any data-owning custom role
options.explanation: is incorrect. STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS = 0 disables statement timeouts (queries run without a time limit), not data access. This does not restrict what data SECURITYADMIN can see.

questions.stem: An organization uses Snowflake's object hierarchy: Organization → Account → Database → Schema → Table. A login policy is created and needs to be assigned to a specific set of users. Which command correctly assigns a login policy to a user?

options.text: ALTER ACCOUNT SET LOGIN_POLICY = strict_login_policy;
options.explanation: is incorrect. ALTER ACCOUNT SET LOGIN_POLICY applies the policy as the account-level default for all users, not to a specific user.

options.text: GRANT LOGIN_POLICY strict_login_policy TO USER john_doe;
options.explanation: is correct. Login policies are assigned to specific users using ALTER USER ... SET LOGIN_POLICY = policy_name. This is the correct syntax for per-user login policy assignment.

options.text: CREATE USER john_doe WITH LOGIN_POLICY = strict_login_policy;
options.explanation: is incorrect. GRANT LOGIN_POLICY is not valid Snowflake syntax. Login policies are assigned via ALTER USER or ALTER ACCOUNT, not granted.

options.text: ALTER USER john_doe SET LOGIN_POLICY = strict_login_policy;
options.explanation: is incorrect. SET LOGIN_POLICY is not a supported parameter in the CREATE USER statement. Login policies must be applied after user creation using ALTER USER.

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator needs to enumerate all users who have not logged in for more than 90 days. Select TWO approaches that can accomplish this. (Select TWO)

options.text: Query SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.USERS and filter where LAST_SUCCESS_LOGIN < DATEADD('day', -90, CURRENT_TIMESTAMP())
options.explanation: is correct. SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.USERS contains user metadata including LAST_SUCCESS_LOGIN. Querying WHERE LAST_SUCCESS_LOGIN < DATEADD('day', -90, CURRENT_TIMESTAMP()) directly identifies users inactive for 90+ days.

options.text: Run SHOW USERS and check the last_success_login column
options.explanation: is correct. SHOW USERS outputs a result set with a last_success_login column for each user, allowing administrators to identify users who have not logged in recently. This works without needing ACCOUNT_USAGE access.

options.text: Query SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.LOGIN_HISTORY and find users with no records in the last 90 days
options.explanation: is partially usable but imprecise. LOGIN_HISTORY records login attempts and can be queried to find users absent from the last 90 days, but users who have never logged in would not appear in LOGIN_HISTORY at all — they would be missed.

options.text: Run SELECT * FROM TABLE(INFORMATION_SCHEMA.USER_HISTORY()) WHERE last_login < DATEADD('day', -90, CURRENT_TIMESTAMP())
options.explanation: is incorrect. INFORMATION_SCHEMA.USER_HISTORY() is not a valid Snowflake function. No such table function exists.

questions.stem: Which Snowflake system role has the ability to manage grants (GRANT/REVOKE privileges) for ALL securable objects in the account, including granting any privilege to any role?

options.text: SYSADMIN
options.explanation: is incorrect. SYSADMIN creates and manages database objects (databases, schemas, tables, warehouses). It can grant privileges on objects it owns, but does not have the account-wide MANAGE GRANTS privilege.

options.text: USERADMIN
options.explanation: is incorrect. USERADMIN can create users and roles but cannot grant arbitrary privileges on objects. Its scope is limited to user and role management.

options.text: SECURITYADMIN
options.explanation: is correct. SECURITYADMIN has the MANAGE GRANTS global privilege, which allows it to grant or revoke any privilege on any securable object in the account. This makes SECURITYADMIN the role responsible for all access control management across the account.

options.text: PUBLIC
options.explanation: is incorrect. PUBLIC is the default role granted to all users with minimal access. It has no administrative privileges.

questions.stem: An administrator wants to see the current credit quota and the amount of credits already consumed for all resource monitors in the account. Which command provides this information?

options.text: SELECT * FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_DAILY_HISTORY;
options.explanation: is incorrect. METERING_DAILY_HISTORY shows overall credit consumption by service type (e.g., virtual warehouses, Snowpipe) aggregated by day. It does not break down by individual resource monitor.

options.text: SELECT * FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.RESOURCE_MONITORS;
options.explanation: is correct. SHOW RESOURCE MONITORS displays all resource monitors defined in the account with their credit quota, used credits, status, frequency, start/end timestamps, and trigger configuration. It is the direct DDL command for viewing resource monitor state.

options.text: DESCRIBE RESOURCE MONITOR all;
options.explanation: is incorrect. There is no SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.RESOURCE_MONITORS view in Snowflake. Resource monitor information is obtained via SHOW RESOURCE MONITORS or the Snowflake web UI.

options.text: SHOW RESOURCE MONITORS;
options.explanation: is incorrect. DESCRIBE RESOURCE MONITOR all is not valid Snowflake syntax. Resource monitors are described individually using DESCRIBE RESOURCE MONITOR monitor_name.

questions.stem: A company wants to set up credit spend controls per department. The Data Engineering department has three warehouses (ETL_WH, STAGING_WH, TRANSFORM_WH) and a monthly budget of 2000 credits. How should the administrator configure resource monitoring?

options.text: Create one resource monitor with CREDIT_QUOTA = 2000, assign all three warehouses to it
options.explanation: is correct. A single resource monitor can be assigned to multiple warehouses. Credits consumed by all assigned warehouses count toward the single shared quota. This is the correct approach for department-level spend control — one monitor with the total budget of 2000 credits, assigned to all three departmental warehouses.

options.text: Create three resource monitors each with CREDIT_QUOTA = 666, one per warehouse
options.explanation: is incorrect. Creating three monitors at 666 credits each means each warehouse operates against its own independent budget; they do not share a combined pool. A single warehouse could exhaust its share while others sit idle.

options.text: Set CREDIT_QUOTA = 2000 at the account level and rely on overall account monitoring
options.explanation: is incorrect. Account-level monitoring applies a budget to all compute across the entire account, not just the Data Engineering department's warehouses.

options.text: Create a BUDGET object with BUDGET_LIMIT = 2000 assigned to the department role
options.explanation: is incorrect. BUDGET objects in Snowflake are a valid feature but BUDGET_LIMIT assigned to a role is not correct BUDGET syntax. Budgets are scoped to specific objects or the account, not to roles.

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator is investigating why a warehouse was unexpectedly suspended mid-day. Select TWO queries that would help determine if a resource monitor triggered the suspension. (Select TWO)

options.text: SHOW RESOURCE MONITORS; — check STATUS and USED_CREDITS vs CREDIT_QUOTA
options.explanation: is correct. SHOW RESOURCE MONITORS shows each monitor's USED_CREDITS vs CREDIT_QUOTA and its STATUS. If a monitor assigned to the warehouse shows STATUS = SUSPENDED and consumed credits at or near quota, this confirms a resource monitor-triggered suspension.

options.text: SELECT * FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY WHERE WAREHOUSE_NAME = 'MY_WH' AND ERROR_CODE IS NOT NULL ORDER BY START_TIME DESC;
options.explanation: is incorrect. QUERY_HISTORY with error codes shows queries that failed or returned errors but does not directly explain why the warehouse itself was suspended. A resource monitor suspension is a warehouse-level event, not a per-query error.

options.text: SELECT * FROM TABLE(INFORMATION_SCHEMA.WAREHOUSE_EVENTS_HISTORY(WAREHOUSE_NAME => 'MY_WH')) WHERE EVENT_STATE = 'SUSPENDED';
options.explanation: is correct. INFORMATION_SCHEMA.WAREHOUSE_EVENTS_HISTORY records warehouse lifecycle events including suspension events with their reason codes. This can directly show whether a resource monitor triggered the suspension and at what time.

options.text: SELECT * FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_DAILY_HISTORY WHERE SERVICE_TYPE = 'WAREHOUSE_METERING' ORDER BY USAGE_DATE DESC;
options.explanation: is incorrect. METERING_DAILY_HISTORY shows aggregate credit consumption by service type per day. It does not track warehouse suspension events or their causes.

questions.stem: A resource monitor is set with FREQUENCY = DAILY and CREDIT_QUOTA = 50. It is now 3 PM and 48 credits have been consumed. The SUSPEND trigger is set at 100%. A new large query is submitted that will consume approximately 5 credits. What happens?

options.text: The query is rejected before starting because submitting it would exceed the daily quota
options.explanation: is incorrect. Snowflake does not pre-emptively reject queries based on predicted future consumption. Queries are not evaluated against remaining quota before being started.

options.text: The query starts and runs; when cumulative consumption hits 50 credits (100%), the warehouse suspends immediately even if the query is still running
options.explanation: is correct. With a SUSPEND trigger (not SUSPEND_IMMEDIATELY), the warehouse allows currently running queries to complete before suspending. The resource monitor detects that the 100% threshold is crossed while the 5-credit query is running and queues the SUSPEND action, but the query finishes first. Only the SUSPEND_IMMEDIATELY trigger would cancel an in-flight query.

options.text: The query is queued until midnight when the daily quota resets
options.explanation: is incorrect. Only the SUSPEND_IMMEDIATELY trigger action cancels the currently executing query when the threshold is crossed. The SUSPEND trigger waits for the current query to complete.

options.text: The query starts and runs; when cumulative consumption hits 50 credits (100%), the warehouse is suspended after the query completes
options.explanation: is incorrect. Snowflake does not queue pending queries until the next reset period when a SUSPEND threshold is hit. The pending queue behavior is unrelated to resource monitor resets.

questions.stem: A company wants more granular credit monitoring than resource monitors provide — specifically, they want to track and alert on spending for individual Snowflake objects like a specific table with automatic clustering enabled. Which Snowflake feature should the administrator use?

options.text: Create a resource monitor with OBJECT_SCOPE = TABLE and assign it to the specific table
options.explanation: is incorrect. Resource monitors cannot be scoped to individual objects like tables. They apply at the warehouse level (to one or more virtual warehouses) or at the account level — not to individual tables.

options.text: Query METERING_DAILY_HISTORY daily with a custom alert threshold in an external monitoring tool
options.explanation: is correct. The BUDGET feature (CREATE BUDGET) provides more granular spending control than resource monitors. Budgets can be scoped to individual Snowflake objects, including tables with automatic clustering, specific accounts, or specific serverless services, allowing per-object credit tracking and alerting.

options.text: Enable CREDIT_TRACKING = TRUE on the table using ALTER TABLE
options.explanation: is incorrect. Querying METERING_DAILY_HISTORY daily and setting custom thresholds in an external tool is a workaround, not a built-in Snowflake alerting feature. It also does not provide object-level granularity.

options.text: Use the BUDGET feature (CREATE BUDGET) to create a budget scoped to the specific object
options.explanation: is incorrect. CREDIT_TRACKING = TRUE on ALTER TABLE is not a valid Snowflake table parameter. No such parameter exists.

questions.stem: A developer reports getting the error 'Object does not exist or operation cannot be performed' when querying a table they believe they have access to. The administrator confirms the developer's role has SELECT on the table. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: The developer needs the REFERENCES privilege in addition to SELECT
options.explanation: is incorrect. REFERENCES is a privilege needed to reference a table in a foreign key constraint definition, not for SELECT queries. Missing REFERENCES does not produce this error.

options.text: The developer's role is missing USAGE privilege on the database or schema containing the table
options.explanation: is correct. In Snowflake, to access an object a role must have USAGE on every container in the hierarchy: USAGE on the database, USAGE on the schema, and then SELECT on the table. The error 'Object does not exist or operation cannot be performed' is Snowflake's generic error when the role cannot see the object due to missing USAGE on a container. The object appears non-existent from the role's perspective.

options.text: The table has been encrypted and the developer needs a decryption key
options.explanation: is incorrect. Snowflake manages encryption transparently at the platform level. Individual tables are not encrypted in a way that requires users to provide a decryption key for SELECT access.

options.text: The developer needs to run USE WAREHOUSE before querying
options.explanation: is incorrect. A missing warehouse produces a different error about no warehouse selected. The 'Object does not exist' error specifically indicates a visibility issue due to missing USAGE on a container.

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator applies a Dynamic Data Masking policy that replaces SSN values with 'XXX-XX-XXXX' for all roles except ANALYST_FULL_ACCESS. The policy is applied to the SSN column in the EMPLOYEES table. A user with both ANALYST_ROLE and ANALYST_FULL_ACCESS roles granted queries the table using the ANALYST_ROLE as their active primary role (USE ROLE ANALYST_ROLE). What does the user see for the SSN column?

options.text: The actual SSN value, because the user has ANALYST_FULL_ACCESS granted to them even if it is not their active role
options.explanation: is incorrect. Snowflake masking policies evaluate CURRENT_ROLE() (or explicitly written policy logic), not all roles granted to the user. Having a role granted does not affect masking if it is not the active role.

options.text: The masked value 'XXX-XX-XXXX', because the masking policy evaluates CURRENT_ROLE() which is ANALYST_ROLE
options.explanation: is correct. Dynamic Data Masking policies use CURRENT_ROLE() to determine what to show. CURRENT_ROLE() returns the currently active primary role, which is ANALYST_ROLE in this scenario. Even though the user has ANALYST_FULL_ACCESS granted, it is not the active role, so the masking condition is not met and the masked value 'XXX-XX-XXXX' is returned.

options.text: The actual SSN value, because Snowflake evaluates all granted roles when applying masking
options.explanation: is incorrect. Snowflake does not evaluate all granted roles for masking decisions by default. Only the active primary role (or secondary roles if explicitly checked in the policy) is evaluated.

options.text: NULL, because the masking policy cannot determine which role to apply
options.explanation: is incorrect. Masking policies return a defined masked value when the condition is not met — they do not return NULL unless specifically coded to do so. The policy here returns 'XXX-XX-XXXX'.

questions.stem: An administrator needs to audit all privileges granted to the REPORTING_ROLE. Select TWO commands that provide different aspects of this audit. (Select TWO)

options.text: SHOW GRANTS TO ROLE REPORTING_ROLE;
options.explanation: is correct. SHOW GRANTS TO ROLE REPORTING_ROLE lists all privileges granted to that role, including privilege type, object type, object name, and the GRANTED_BY column — providing a complete view of what the role can do.

options.text: SHOW GRANTS OF ROLE REPORTING_ROLE;
options.explanation: is correct. SHOW GRANTS OF ROLE REPORTING_ROLE shows which users and roles have been granted this role (i.e., who holds REPORTING_ROLE in their hierarchy). Together with option A, this provides a complete picture of what REPORTING_ROLE can do and who uses it.

options.text: SELECT * FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.GRANTS_TO_ROLES WHERE GRANTEE_NAME = 'REPORTING_ROLE';
options.explanation: is partially valid. ACCOUNT_USAGE.GRANTS_TO_ROLES provides similar data to SHOW GRANTS TO ROLE but with up to 45-minute latency. It is useful for historical auditing but slower than the SHOW command for immediate audits.

options.text: DESCRIBE ROLE REPORTING_ROLE PRIVILEGES;
options.explanation: is incorrect. DESCRIBE ROLE REPORTING_ROLE PRIVILEGES is not valid Snowflake syntax. Role privileges cannot be described with this command.

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator is troubleshooting a future grant issue. The command GRANT SELECT ON FUTURE TABLES IN SCHEMA ANALYTICS TO ROLE BI_ROLE was run 2 months ago. A new table ANALYTICS.NEW_METRICS was created last week. The BI_ROLE cannot SELECT from NEW_METRICS. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: Future grants only apply to tables created within 24 hours of the GRANT command
options.explanation: is incorrect. Future grants do not expire after 24 hours. They persist indefinitely until explicitly revoked.

options.text: The table was created by a role different from the one that owns the schema, and future grants do not apply in this case
options.explanation: is incorrect. Future grants apply to all new tables in the schema regardless of which role created the table. The creator role does not affect future grant applicability.

options.text: The future grant was revoked or a REVOKE ALL PRIVILEGES ON FUTURE TABLES was issued, removing the future grant
options.explanation: is correct. Future grants persist indefinitely — they do not expire after 24 hours or 30 days. The most common reason a future grant stops working is that it was explicitly revoked with REVOKE SELECT ON FUTURE TABLES IN SCHEMA ANALYTICS FROM ROLE BI_ROLE, or a blanket REVOKE ALL PRIVILEGES ON FUTURE TABLES was issued. Run SHOW FUTURE GRANTS IN SCHEMA ANALYTICS to verify.

options.text: Future grants require re-issuance every 30 days to remain active
options.explanation: is incorrect. Future grants do not require periodic re-issuance. Once issued, they remain active until explicitly revoked.

questions.stem: A user activates secondary roles by running USE SECONDARY ROLES ALL. The user's primary role is DEVELOPER_ROLE. The user also has ANALYST_ROLE granted. ANALYST_ROLE has SELECT on a sensitive table. DEVELOPER_ROLE does not. Which statement about this query scenario is correct?

options.text: The query will fail because primary role DEVELOPER_ROLE does not have SELECT on the table
options.explanation: is incorrect. Given that USE SECONDARY ROLES ALL has been activated, the primary role's limitations are supplemented by all secondary roles. The query succeeds using ANALYST_ROLE's SELECT privilege.

options.text: The query will succeed because USE SECONDARY ROLES ALL activates all granted roles simultaneously, and ANALYST_ROLE's SELECT is usable
options.explanation: is correct. USE SECONDARY ROLES ALL activates all roles granted to the user simultaneously during the session. With this enabled, the user can use privileges from any of their granted roles, not just the active primary role. ANALYST_ROLE's SELECT privilege on the sensitive table becomes available even though DEVELOPER_ROLE (the primary role) does not have it.

options.text: The query will succeed only if DEVELOPER_ROLE is a parent role of ANALYST_ROLE in the hierarchy
options.explanation: is incorrect. Secondary roles do not require a parent-child hierarchy relationship. USE SECONDARY ROLES ALL activates all granted roles regardless of their relationship to the primary role.

options.text: USE SECONDARY ROLES ALL only applies to DDL operations, not DML like SELECT
options.explanation: is incorrect. Secondary roles apply to all SQL operations including DML (SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE), not only DDL operations.

questions.stem: An administrator creates a network policy and wants to apply it to the entire Snowflake account. Which command correctly applies the policy at the account level?

options.text: ALTER ACCOUNT SET NETWORK_POLICY = my_corp_policy;
options.explanation: is correct. The correct command to apply a network policy at the account level is ALTER ACCOUNT SET NETWORK_POLICY = policy_name. This restricts all account access based on the policy's IP allow and block rules.

options.text: GRANT NETWORK_POLICY my_corp_policy TO ACCOUNT;
options.explanation: is incorrect. GRANT NETWORK_POLICY is not valid Snowflake syntax. Network policies are applied via ALTER ACCOUNT or ALTER USER, not granted.

options.text: CREATE NETWORK POLICY my_corp_policy ACCOUNT_LEVEL = TRUE;
options.explanation: is incorrect. CREATE NETWORK POLICY does not support an ACCOUNT_LEVEL parameter. The policy is created separately and then applied using ALTER ACCOUNT.

options.text: ALTER NETWORK POLICY my_corp_policy SET SCOPE = ACCOUNT;
options.explanation: is incorrect. ALTER NETWORK POLICY SET SCOPE is not valid Snowflake syntax. Scope is determined by where the policy is applied (ALTER ACCOUNT vs ALTER USER), not by a property on the policy itself.

questions.reference: [Controlling Network Traffic with Network Policies | Snowflake Documentation](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/network-policies)

questions.stem: A company has implemented network rules using the newer network rule construct. They want to allow access only from specific AWS VPC endpoints. Which type of network rule should be used?

options.text: TYPE = IPV4 with the VPC CIDR range
options.explanation: is incorrect. TYPE = IPV4 uses IP addresses or CIDR ranges and applies to public internet traffic — it cannot identify VPC-internal private traffic by VPC endpoint.

options.text: TYPE = AWSVPCEID with the VPC endpoint IDs
options.explanation: is correct. Network rules support TYPE = AWSVPCEID, which allows specifying AWS VPC endpoint IDs to restrict access to traffic originating from specific VPC endpoints. This ensures traffic flows only from approved VPCs via PrivateLink endpoints.

options.text: TYPE = HOST with the VPC DNS hostname
options.explanation: is incorrect. TYPE = HOST uses DNS hostnames and is used for external function and API integration configurations, not for VPC endpoint-based access restriction.

options.text: TYPE = PRIVATELINK with the endpoint service name
options.explanation: is incorrect. TYPE = PRIVATELINK is not a valid network rule type. The correct type for VPC endpoint-based restrictions is AWSVPCEID.

questions.stem: A security team requires MFA for all interactive users but wants service accounts to bypass MFA for automation. Select TWO approaches to implement this in Snowflake. (Select TWO)

options.text: Set up MFA enrollment for all users, then configure service account users to use key pair authentication instead of password-based login (which bypasses MFA)
options.explanation: is correct. Key pair authentication for service accounts is the standard approach. Service accounts authenticate via RSA keys and bypass the password/MFA flow entirely. MFA only applies to username/password authentication, so key pair service accounts are inherently exempt.

options.text: Create a login policy with MFA_ENROLLMENT = REQUIRED and apply it at the account level, then create a separate login policy with MFA_ENROLLMENT = OPTIONAL and assign it to service account users
options.explanation: is correct. Login policies support MFA_ENROLLMENT settings. Applying an account-level login policy with MFA_ENROLLMENT = REQUIRED and then assigning a separate user-level login policy with MFA_ENROLLMENT = OPTIONAL to service account users is the correct per-user MFA override mechanism.

options.text: Enable MFA at the account level and then run ALTER USER svc_account DISABLE MFA = TRUE for each service account
options.explanation: is incorrect. ALTER USER svc_account DISABLE MFA = TRUE is not valid Snowflake syntax. There is no DISABLE MFA parameter on ALTER USER.

options.text: Use a network policy to restrict service accounts to specific IP ranges, then exempt those IPs from MFA requirements
options.explanation: is incorrect. Network policies control which IP addresses can connect; they cannot exempt specific IPs from MFA requirements. MFA enforcement is controlled by login policies, not network policies.

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator is reviewing a security concern: a user's RSA_PUBLIC_KEY was compromised. The user currently has both RSA_PUBLIC_KEY (compromised) and RSA_PUBLIC_KEY_2 (valid and in use by the application) set. What is the immediate action to revoke the compromised key without disrupting the running application?

options.text: ALTER USER affected_user UNSET RSA_PUBLIC_KEY;
options.explanation: is correct. Since RSA_PUBLIC_KEY (slot 1) is the compromised key and RSA_PUBLIC_KEY_2 (slot 2) is the valid key in use, the correct action is ALTER USER affected_user UNSET RSA_PUBLIC_KEY. This removes only slot 1, leaving RSA_PUBLIC_KEY_2 intact so the application continues authenticating normally.

options.text: ALTER USER affected_user UNSET RSA_PUBLIC_KEY_2;
options.explanation: is incorrect. UNSET RSA_PUBLIC_KEY_2 would remove the valid key currently used by the application, disrupting the running service.

options.text: ALTER USER affected_user SET RSA_PUBLIC_KEY = NULL;
options.explanation: is incorrect. SET RSA_PUBLIC_KEY = NULL is not the supported syntax for removing a key. The correct approach is UNSET RSA_PUBLIC_KEY.

options.text: DROP USER affected_user; CREATE USER affected_user RSA_PUBLIC_KEY_2 = '<valid_key>';
options.explanation: is incorrect. Dropping and recreating the user account is highly disruptive — it destroys all role grants, session history, and other user metadata. This is never the appropriate response to key revocation.

questions.stem: A warehouse is configured with MIN_CLUSTER_COUNT = 1 and MAX_CLUSTER_COUNT = 4, with SCALING_POLICY = STANDARD. When does Snowflake add additional clusters?

options.text: When a single query exceeds a configurable execution time threshold
options.explanation: is incorrect. Multi-cluster warehouse scaling is not triggered by individual query execution time. A single long-running query does not cause additional clusters to start.

options.text: At a fixed schedule defined by the administrator
options.explanation: is correct. In a multi-cluster warehouse, Snowflake's auto-scaling adds additional clusters when it detects query queuing — queries waiting for an available compute slot. With SCALING_POLICY = STANDARD, Snowflake scales out promptly when queuing is detected. SCALING_POLICY = ECONOMY waits longer before adding clusters.

options.text: Only when manually triggered using ALTER WAREHOUSE RESUME CLUSTER
options.explanation: is incorrect. Snowflake auto-scaling does not use administrator-defined schedules. Scaling decisions are dynamic and based on real-time queue depth.

options.text: When queries are queuing and the system determines additional clusters would reduce wait times
options.explanation: is incorrect. With auto-scaling configured (MIN_CLUSTER_COUNT and MAX_CLUSTER_COUNT set), additional clusters start automatically. Manual intervention is not required.

questions.stem: An administrator wants to minimize idle warehouse costs for a warehouse used only during business hours (8 AM to 6 PM). The warehouse should not be running overnight. Which configuration is MOST appropriate?

options.text: Set AUTO_SUSPEND = 60 and AUTO_RESUME = TRUE; schedule a task to resume at 8 AM and suspend at 6 PM
options.explanation: is incorrect. Adding a scheduled task to resume at 8 AM and suspend at 6 PM is operationally complex and unnecessary. AUTO_SUSPEND handles overnight shutdown automatically without task scheduling.

options.text: Set AUTO_SUSPEND = 60 and AUTO_RESUME = TRUE; rely on auto-suspend to handle overnight shutdown automatically
options.explanation: is correct. With AUTO_SUSPEND = 60 and AUTO_RESUME = TRUE, the warehouse automatically suspends 60 seconds after the last query completes (shortly after the last business-hours query), and automatically resumes when users submit queries the next morning. This is the simplest and most cost-effective configuration.

options.text: Set AUTO_SUSPEND = 28800 (8 hours) to ensure the warehouse stays running all day
options.explanation: is incorrect. AUTO_SUSPEND = 28800 means the warehouse stays running for 8 hours after the last query — it could run all night if the last query was at 6 PM, wasting significant credits.

options.text: Set INITIALLY_SUSPENDED = TRUE and manually start the warehouse each morning
options.explanation: is incorrect. INITIALLY_SUSPENDED with manual start requires someone to manually start the warehouse every morning, which is operationally burdensome and prone to human error.

questions.stem: A large table is showing degraded query performance after months of continuous INSERT operations. Clustering depth has increased significantly. Select TWO actions that can help restore clustering efficiency. (Select TWO)

options.text: Enable automatic clustering with ALTER TABLE orders CLUSTER BY (order_date, region);
options.explanation: is correct. Enabling automatic clustering (ALTER TABLE orders CLUSTER BY (order_date, region)) tells Snowflake to continuously maintain clustering in the background. The automatic clustering service reclusters micro-partitions as DML operations degrade clustering depth.

options.text: Run ALTER TABLE orders RECLUSTER to manually trigger a one-time reclustering operation
options.explanation: is incorrect. ALTER TABLE orders RECLUSTER is not valid Snowflake syntax. There is no manual RECLUSTER command. When automatic clustering is enabled, Snowflake manages reclustering automatically.

options.text: Increase the warehouse size to compensate for the poor clustering
options.explanation: is incorrect. Increasing the warehouse size may marginally speed up queries that scan poorly clustered data, but it does not fix the clustering problem — the underlying micro-partition layout remains inefficient.

options.text: Drop and recreate the table with data loaded in sorted order by the clustering key
options.explanation: is correct. Dropping and recreating the table with data loaded in sorted order by the clustering key achieves a perfectly clustered initial state. While it requires downtime and a full reload, it immediately restores clustering efficiency.

questions.stem: A table has TIME_TRAVEL_RETENTION = 30 days. A user accidentally drops the table. After 25 days, the administrator discovers the dropped table. Can the table be recovered, and how?

options.text: Yes, using UNDROP TABLE table_name; because the table is within the 30-day retention period
options.explanation: is correct. UNDROP TABLE is the correct command to recover a dropped table within its Time Travel retention period. When a table is dropped, Snowflake retains it in a 'dropped' state for the DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS duration. Since 25 days is within the 30-day retention window, UNDROP TABLE table_name restores the table.

options.text: No, dropped tables cannot be recovered using Time Travel; only DML changes can be rolled back
options.explanation: is incorrect. Time Travel applies to DROP TABLE operations as well as DML changes. Dropped tables can be recovered using UNDROP TABLE as long as they are within the retention window.

options.text: Yes, using CREATE TABLE table_name CLONE table_name AT (OFFSET => -2160000);
options.explanation: is incorrect. CREATE TABLE ... CLONE ... AT (OFFSET) clones a table to a historical state — it is used to access data at a past point in time, not to recover a dropped table. UNDROP TABLE is the correct command.

options.text: No, because Time Travel only applies to table data modifications, not DROP TABLE operations
options.explanation: is incorrect. Time Travel applies specifically to DROP TABLE operations — this is one of its documented use cases. UNDROP TABLE restores dropped tables, schemas, and databases.

questions.stem: A compliance officer needs to verify that all login attempts (both successful and failed) from external applications have been logged for the past 30 days. Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view and key columns should the administrator reference?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY — columns: USER_NAME, START_TIME, EXECUTION_STATUS
options.explanation: is incorrect. QUERY_HISTORY covers SQL queries executed during sessions. It does not track login attempts — only activity after a session is established.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.ACCESS_HISTORY — columns: USER_NAME, QUERY_START_TIME, OBJECTS_ACCESSED
options.explanation: is correct. SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.LOGIN_HISTORY is the correct view for auditing authentication events. Key columns include USER_NAME, EVENT_TIMESTAMP, IS_SUCCESS (whether login succeeded), ERROR_MESSAGE (failure reason), and CLIENT_TYPE/CLIENT_APPLICATION_ID (the connecting application). This captures both successful and failed login attempts.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.SESSIONS — columns: USER_NAME, CREATED_ON, CLIENT_APPLICATION_ID
options.explanation: is incorrect. ACCESS_HISTORY tracks which data objects were accessed during query execution. It does not record login or authentication events.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.LOGIN_HISTORY — columns: USER_NAME, EVENT_TIMESTAMP, IS_SUCCESS, ERROR_MESSAGE, CLIENT_TYPE
options.explanation: is incorrect. SESSIONS tracks active session metadata. It shows established sessions but does not capture failed login attempts or authentication event history.

questions.stem: A data governance team wants to track data lineage — specifically which downstream queries and users accessed data that originated from a source table PROD.SALES.TRANSACTIONS. Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view supports column-level lineage tracking?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY joined with EXECUTION_PLAN
options.explanation: is incorrect. QUERY_HISTORY contains query text and execution metadata but not structured lineage. There is no EXECUTION_PLAN join that provides column-level lineage.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.ACCESS_HISTORY with DIRECT_OBJECTS_ACCESSED and BASE_OBJECTS_ACCESSED columns
options.explanation: is correct. ACCESS_HISTORY provides the most detailed lineage in Snowflake. DIRECT_OBJECTS_ACCESSED shows the immediate objects a query accessed, while BASE_OBJECTS_ACCESSED shows underlying base tables even when queries went through views. Combined, these columns enable column-level data lineage tracking — tracing which users accessed specific columns from a source table, even indirectly.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.OBJECT_DEPENDENCIES
options.explanation: is incorrect. OBJECT_DEPENDENCIES tracks static schema-level dependencies (e.g., a view depends on a table) but does not capture runtime access lineage — it does not show which users accessed data.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.POLICY_REFERENCES filtered by POLICY_KIND = 'LINEAGE'
options.explanation: is incorrect. POLICY_REFERENCES tracks which masking or row access policies are applied to which objects. POLICY_KIND = 'LINEAGE' is not a valid filter value, and POLICY_REFERENCES does not track data access lineage.

options.text: ALTER TABLE employees ALTER COLUMN ssn SET MASKING POLICY new_ssn_mask; (Directly replaces the existing policy)
options.explanation: is correct. In Snowflake, you can directly SET a new masking policy on a column that already has one — the new SET replaces the existing policy assignment in a single atomic operation. This is the simplest and most efficient approach.

options.text: ALTER TABLE employees ALTER COLUMN ssn UNSET MASKING POLICY; then ALTER TABLE employees ALTER COLUMN ssn SET MASKING POLICY new_ssn_mask;
options.explanation: is partially correct. UNSET followed by SET also works but requires two separate commands. Option A (direct SET) is simpler and preferred.

options.text: DROP MASKING POLICY ssn_mask; then ALTER TABLE employees ALTER COLUMN ssn SET MASKING POLICY new_ssn_mask;
options.explanation: is incorrect. Dropping the old policy would fail if it is still applied to any column — you would first need to UNSET it from all columns. Dropping the policy is not needed to replace its assignment.

options.text: ALTER TABLE employees ALTER COLUMN ssn REPLACE MASKING POLICY ssn_mask WITH new_ssn_mask;
options.explanation: is incorrect. REPLACE MASKING POLICY ... WITH ... is not valid Snowflake syntax. The REPLACE keyword is not used in masking policy assignments.

questions.stem: An administrator creates a resource monitor: CREATE RESOURCE MONITOR dept_monitor WITH CREDIT_QUOTA = 1000 FREQUENCY = MONTHLY START_TIMESTAMP = IMMEDIATELY TRIGGERS ON 90 PERCENT DO SUSPEND ON 100 PERCENT DO SUSPEND_IMMEDIATELY; The monitor is assigned to PROD_WH. Three days before month end, the warehouse hits 100% and is suspended. The business needs the warehouse running urgently. What must the administrator do?

options.text: Wait until the start of next month when the monthly quota resets automatically
options.explanation: is incorrect. Waiting until month end is not an option when there is an urgent business need. Additionally, the quota increase approach does not require waiting.

options.text: Increase the CREDIT_QUOTA on the resource monitor, then resume the warehouse
options.explanation: is correct. When a warehouse is suspended by a resource monitor that has hit 100% quota, increasing the CREDIT_QUOTA (e.g., ALTER RESOURCE MONITOR dept_monitor SET CREDIT_QUOTA = 1200) removes the quota exceedance, and the administrator can then resume the warehouse with ALTER WAREHOUSE PROD_WH RESUME.

options.text: Run ALTER WAREHOUSE PROD_WH RESUME — the warehouse can be manually resumed even when suspended by a resource monitor
options.explanation: is partially true — ALTER WAREHOUSE PROD_WH RESUME briefly resumes the warehouse — but the resource monitor immediately re-suspends it because the quota is still exceeded. Without first increasing the quota, resume alone is ineffective.

options.text: Delete the resource monitor assignment, resume the warehouse, and reassign the monitor next month
options.explanation: is incorrect. Removing the monitor assignment and reattaching next month removes cost controls entirely and is an overly disruptive approach to an urgent operational need.

questions.stem: An administrator sets ALTER ACCOUNT SET LOCK_TIMEOUT = 7200. A concurrent DML operation on table ORDERS holds a lock. A second transaction waits for the lock. What happens after 7200 seconds if the lock has not been released?

options.text: The second transaction is automatically cancelled with a lock timeout error
options.explanation: is correct. LOCK_TIMEOUT specifies how long a statement waits to acquire a lock before being cancelled. After 7200 seconds, the waiting (second) transaction is cancelled with a lock timeout error. The lock-holding transaction continues unaffected. The cancelled statement can be retried.

options.text: The first transaction (lock holder) is forcibly rolled back to free the lock
options.explanation: is incorrect. LOCK_TIMEOUT does not force the lock holder to roll back. Only the waiting statement is cancelled; the transaction holding the lock proceeds normally.

options.text: Both transactions are suspended until an administrator manually resolves the conflict
options.explanation: is incorrect. LOCK_TIMEOUT causes the waiting transaction to be cancelled, not suspended. There is no manual administrator intervention required — the timeout is automatic.

options.text: The second transaction continues waiting indefinitely because LOCK_TIMEOUT does not apply to DML locks
options.explanation: is incorrect. LOCK_TIMEOUT applies to DML locks. After the configured period, the waiting statement is cancelled rather than waiting indefinitely.

questions.stem: An administrator wants to check the effective value of the STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS parameter for a specific warehouse and understand whether it was set at the account, warehouse, or session level. Which command provides this level detail?

options.text: SHOW PARAMETERS LIKE 'STATEMENT_TIMEOUT%' IN WAREHOUSE my_wh;
options.explanation: is correct. SHOW PARAMETERS LIKE 'STATEMENT_TIMEOUT%' IN WAREHOUSE warehouse_name shows parameters at the warehouse level, including whether the effective value was set at the warehouse level or inherited from the account. The output includes a 'level' column showing the source of the effective value.

options.text: DESCRIBE WAREHOUSE my_wh PARAMETERS;
options.explanation: is incorrect. DESCRIBE WAREHOUSE ... PARAMETERS is not valid Snowflake syntax. DESCRIBE WAREHOUSE shows structural properties of the warehouse, not session or account parameters.

options.text: SELECT * FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_PARAMETERS WHERE WAREHOUSE_NAME = 'MY_WH';
options.explanation: is incorrect. There is no SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_PARAMETERS view. Parameter values are not exposed through ACCOUNT_USAGE as a queryable view.

options.text: SHOW WAREHOUSES LIKE 'MY_WH' PARAMETERS;
options.explanation: is incorrect. SHOW WAREHOUSES LIKE 'MY_WH' PARAMETERS is not valid Snowflake syntax. SHOW WAREHOUSES shows warehouse configuration, not parameter values.

questions.stem: A user contacts support because they are unable to log into Snowflake and receive the message 'User is locked'. The administrator confirms the user hit the maximum failed login attempts. Which command immediately restores login access for this user?

options.text: ALTER USER locked_user SET DISABLED = FALSE;
options.explanation: is incorrect. DISABLED = FALSE re-enables a user who was explicitly disabled by an administrator using SET DISABLED = TRUE. This is a different state from login lockout caused by failed authentication attempts.

options.text: ALTER USER locked_user UNLOCK;
options.explanation: is incorrect. ALTER USER locked_user UNLOCK is not valid Snowflake syntax. The correct command uses SET MINS_TO_UNLOCK = 0.

options.text: ALTER USER locked_user SET MINS_TO_UNLOCK = 0;
options.explanation: is correct. ALTER USER locked_user SET MINS_TO_UNLOCK = 0 immediately clears the lockout by reducing the remaining lock duration to zero. This is the standard Snowflake method for unlocking a user account after failed login lockout.

options.text: GRANT LOGIN TO USER locked_user;
options.explanation: is incorrect. GRANT LOGIN TO USER is not valid Snowflake syntax. Login access is controlled through the user enabled/disabled state and lockout parameters, not through a GRANT statement.

questions.stem: A Snowflake user logs in but complains they have to manually run USE ROLE and USE WAREHOUSE every session. The administrator wants to configure the user to automatically use REPORTING_ROLE and REPORTING_WH. Which ALTER USER parameters should be set?

options.text: ALTER USER user1 SET DEFAULT_ROLE = 'REPORTING_ROLE' DEFAULT_WAREHOUSE = 'REPORTING_WH';
options.explanation: is correct. DEFAULT_ROLE and DEFAULT_WAREHOUSE are the valid Snowflake user properties that automatically configure the role and warehouse at session start. When the user logs in, Snowflake activates DEFAULT_ROLE (if granted to the user) and sets DEFAULT_WAREHOUSE without requiring manual USE commands.

options.text: ALTER USER user1 SET AUTO_ROLE = 'REPORTING_ROLE' AUTO_WAREHOUSE = 'REPORTING_WH';
options.explanation: is incorrect. AUTO_ROLE and AUTO_WAREHOUSE are not valid Snowflake user parameters. These property names do not exist in the ALTER USER syntax.

options.text: ALTER USER user1 SET SESSION_ROLE = 'REPORTING_ROLE' SESSION_WAREHOUSE = 'REPORTING_WH';
options.explanation: is incorrect. SESSION_ROLE and SESSION_WAREHOUSE are not valid ALTER USER parameter names. These do not exist as user-level properties in Snowflake.

options.text: ALTER USER user1 SET LOGIN_ROLE = 'REPORTING_ROLE' LOGIN_WAREHOUSE = 'REPORTING_WH';
options.explanation: is incorrect. LOGIN_ROLE and LOGIN_WAREHOUSE are not valid Snowflake user parameters. The correct names are DEFAULT_ROLE and DEFAULT_WAREHOUSE.

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator wants to add governance documentation to all custom roles, noting the team that owns each role. The FINANCE_TEAM_ROLE needs the comment 'Owned by Finance Operations team'. Which command is correct?

options.text: COMMENT ON ROLE finance_team_role IS 'Owned by Finance Operations team';
options.explanation: is incorrect. COMMENT ON ROLE ... IS is a SQL standard syntax not used in Snowflake for role objects. Snowflake uses ALTER ROLE ... SET COMMENT instead.

options.text: UPDATE ROLE finance_team_role SET DESCRIPTION = 'Owned by Finance Operations team';
options.explanation: is correct. ALTER ROLE role_name SET COMMENT = 'text' is the correct Snowflake DDL to add or update the comment on a role. This comment is visible in SHOW ROLES output and in the Snowflake web UI, supporting governance and role documentation.

options.text: ALTER ROLE finance_team_role MODIFY COMMENT 'Owned by Finance Operations team';
options.explanation: is incorrect. UPDATE ROLE is not valid Snowflake syntax. DML UPDATE does not apply to Snowflake account objects.

options.text: ALTER ROLE finance_team_role SET COMMENT = 'Owned by Finance Operations team';
options.explanation: is incorrect. ALTER ROLE ... MODIFY COMMENT is not valid Snowflake syntax. The correct form uses SET COMMENT =.

questions.stem: A Snowflake task is calling a stored procedure. Inside the stored procedure, the code needs to conditionally execute different logic depending on which task triggered it. Which function allows the procedure to identify its calling task?

options.text: CURRENT_STATEMENT()
options.explanation: is incorrect. CURRENT_STATEMENT() returns the SQL text of the currently executing statement, not the name of the calling task. It is not task-aware.

options.text: SYSTEM$CURRENT_USER_TASK_NAME()
options.explanation: is correct. SYSTEM$CURRENT_USER_TASK_NAME() returns the fully-qualified name of the task currently executing the stored procedure. This enables branching logic inside shared procedures that are called by multiple different tasks.

options.text: INVOKER_TASK_NAME()
options.explanation: is incorrect. INVOKER_TASK_NAME() is not a valid Snowflake function. The correct function is SYSTEM$CURRENT_USER_TASK_NAME().

options.text: SYSTEM$GET_TASK_CONTEXT('task_name')
options.explanation: is incorrect. SYSTEM$GET_TASK_CONTEXT('task_name') is not a valid Snowflake system function. This function does not exist.

questions.stem: An infrastructure-as-code pipeline creates roles as part of environment setup. The pipeline runs on every deployment. Which role creation pattern prevents errors when the role already exists from a previous deployment?

options.text: CREATE OR REPLACE ROLE env_role;
options.explanation: is incorrect. CREATE OR REPLACE ROLE drops and recreates the role, destroying all existing grant assignments in the process. This is dangerous in a re-run pipeline where the role may already have privileges assigned.

options.text: CREATE ROLE env_role IF NOT FOUND;
options.explanation: is incorrect. CREATE ROLE ... IF NOT FOUND is not valid Snowflake syntax. The correct form is IF NOT EXISTS.

options.text: CREATE ROLE IF NOT EXISTS env_role;
options.explanation: is correct. CREATE ROLE IF NOT EXISTS is the idempotent form that creates the role only if it does not already exist. If the role already exists, the statement succeeds without error and without modifying the existing role or its grants — making it safe for IaC pipelines that run on every deployment.

options.text: UPSERT ROLE env_role;
options.explanation: is incorrect. UPSERT ROLE is not valid Snowflake syntax. There is no UPSERT DDL command for roles.

questions.stem: An administrator needs to quickly find all roles that include 'ENGINEER' in their name. Which Snowflake command accomplishes this?

options.text: SELECT ROLE_NAME FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.ROLES WHERE ROLE_NAME ILIKE '%ENGINEER%';
options.explanation: is also valid. ACCOUNT_USAGE.ROLES with ILIKE '%ENGINEER%' also works and supports case-insensitive matching. However, SHOW ROLES LIKE is the direct DDL approach and does not require querying ACCOUNT_USAGE.

options.text: SHOW ROLES LIKE '%ENGINEER%';
options.explanation: is correct. SHOW ROLES LIKE '%ENGINEER%' uses SQL LIKE wildcard pattern matching to filter roles whose names contain 'ENGINEER' anywhere. The % wildcard matches any sequence of characters before and after.

options.text: DESCRIBE ROLES CONTAINING 'ENGINEER';
options.explanation: is incorrect. DESCRIBE ROLES CONTAINING 'ENGINEER' is not valid Snowflake syntax. DESCRIBE can be used on individual objects but not with a CONTAINING filter.

options.text: LIST ROLES FILTER NAME = '*ENGINEER*';
options.explanation: is incorrect. LIST ROLES FILTER NAME = '*ENGINEER*' is not valid Snowflake syntax. LIST is used for stage file listing, not for role management.

questions.stem: Snowflake is preparing to enable behavior change bundle '2025_01' for all accounts. An administrator wants to test the impact in a sandbox account before it becomes mandatory. Which system function enables the bundle in the sandbox?

options.text: ALTER ACCOUNT ENABLE BEHAVIOR_CHANGE_BUNDLE = '2025_01';
options.explanation: is incorrect. ALTER ACCOUNT ENABLE BEHAVIOR_CHANGE_BUNDLE = '2025_01' is not valid Snowflake DDL syntax. The correct method uses the SYSTEM$ function.

options.text: SET SNOWFLAKE_BCR_BUNDLE = '2025_01';
options.explanation: is correct. SELECT SYSTEM$ENABLE_BEHAVIOR_CHANGE_BUNDLE('bundle_name') is the Snowflake system function used to opt an account into a specific behavior change bundle before it is automatically enabled account-wide. This allows testing in a sandbox account.

options.text: CREATE BEHAVIOR_CHANGE_TEST FOR BUNDLE '2025_01';
options.explanation: is incorrect. SET SNOWFLAKE_BCR_BUNDLE = '2025_01' is not a valid Snowflake session parameter. No such parameter exists.

options.text: SELECT SYSTEM$ENABLE_BEHAVIOR_CHANGE_BUNDLE('2025_01');
options.explanation: is incorrect. CREATE BEHAVIOR_CHANGE_TEST is not a valid Snowflake DDL command. No such object type exists.

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator creates a resource monitor with NOTIFY_USERS = ('DBA_ADMIN', 'CFO_USER') and a NOTIFY trigger at 80%. The CFO_USER does not have a Snowflake email address configured. What happens when the 80% threshold is crossed?

options.text: The notification fails entirely because one listed user has no email address
options.explanation: is incorrect. A missing email for one user does not cause the entire notification to fail. Other users with valid emails still receive the alert.

options.text: Snowflake notifies all ACCOUNTADMIN users instead since the NOTIFY_USERS list is invalid
options.explanation: is correct. Resource monitor notifications are sent on a best-effort per-user basis. Users in NOTIFY_USERS who have a valid email configured receive the notification; users without an email address configured are silently skipped. The notification failure for one user does not block delivery to others, and the resource monitor continues functioning normally.

options.text: The resource monitor is disabled until all NOTIFY_USERS have valid email addresses
options.explanation: is incorrect. When a NOTIFY_USERS entry lacks an email address, Snowflake does not fall back to notifying all ACCOUNTADMIN users. The absent-email user is simply skipped.

options.text: Snowflake sends the notification to DBA_ADMIN only; CFO_USER is silently skipped
options.explanation: is incorrect. A resource monitor is not disabled due to missing email addresses in NOTIFY_USERS. The monitor continues enforcing thresholds and suspending warehouses as configured.

questions.stem: A resource monitor is assigned to a warehouse and the SUSPEND trigger fires at 100% quota. The administrator immediately increases the quota with ALTER RESOURCE MONITOR mon SET CREDIT_QUOTA = 1500. What additional step is required to resume the warehouse?

options.text: No additional step is needed; increasing the quota automatically resumes the warehouse
options.explanation: is incorrect. Increasing the quota does not trigger an automatic warehouse resume. Snowflake does not monitor the quota increase and restart suspended warehouses automatically.

options.text: Run ALTER WAREHOUSE wh RESUME to manually restart the suspended warehouse
options.explanation: is correct. Increasing the CREDIT_QUOTA on the resource monitor removes the reason for suspension, but it does not automatically resume the warehouse. The administrator must explicitly run ALTER WAREHOUSE wh RESUME to restart the suspended warehouse.

options.text: Wait for the next monitoring cycle (up to 1 hour) when the warehouse auto-resumes
options.explanation: is incorrect. There is no periodic auto-resume cycle for warehouses suspended by a resource monitor. Once suspended, a warehouse stays suspended until manually resumed (after the quota issue is resolved).

options.text: Detach the resource monitor from the warehouse, resume it, then reattach the monitor
options.explanation: is incorrect. Detaching the resource monitor, resuming, and reattaching also works operationally, but it is unnecessarily complex compared to simply increasing the quota and running ALTER WAREHOUSE RESUME.

questions.stem: An administrator wants to understand query queuing pressure on ANALYTICS_WH over the past week. Select TWO data sources that provide this insight. (Select TWO)

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_LOAD_MONITORING — provides 5-minute interval snapshots of queued, running, and blocked query counts
options.explanation: is correct. WAREHOUSE_LOAD_MONITORING provides 5-minute interval snapshots of running, queued, and blocked query counts for each warehouse — ideal for identifying queuing patterns and peak periods over time.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY — QUEUED_OVERLOAD_TIME column shows how long individual queries waited due to warehouse overload
options.explanation: is correct. QUERY_HISTORY has a QUEUED_OVERLOAD_TIME column that shows, per individual query, how long it waited due to warehouse overload. This enables per-query queuing analysis and correlation with specific time periods.

options.text: SHOW RESOURCE MONITORS — the QUEUE_DEPTH column shows historical queue depth per warehouse
options.explanation: is incorrect. SHOW RESOURCE MONITORS does not have a QUEUE_DEPTH column. It shows resource monitor credit quotas and statuses, not per-warehouse query queue metrics.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY — the QUEUED_QUERIES column shows total queuing events
options.explanation: is incorrect. WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY tracks credit consumption per warehouse over time. It does not have a QUEUED_QUERIES column or any queue depth metrics.

questions.stem: A company's storage costs have unexpectedly tripled over three months. Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view provides a time-series history of daily storage usage that can be charted to identify the trend?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.TABLE_STORAGE_METRICS
options.explanation: is incorrect. TABLE_STORAGE_METRICS in ACCOUNT_USAGE provides current storage per table at a point in time, not a historical daily time-series that can be charted for trend analysis.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLE_STORAGE_METRICS
options.explanation: is correct. ACCOUNT_USAGE.STORAGE_USAGE provides daily snapshots of total storage consumed by the account, including AVERAGE_DATABASE_BYTES, AVERAGE_STAGE_BYTES, and AVERAGE_FAILSAFE_BYTES. With 1 year of retention, it can be queried to chart storage growth trends over months.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.DATABASE_REPLICATION_USAGE_HISTORY
options.explanation: is incorrect. INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLE_STORAGE_METRICS shows current table-level storage state, not historical trends. INFORMATION_SCHEMA data has no history retention.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.STORAGE_USAGE
options.explanation: is incorrect. DATABASE_REPLICATION_USAGE_HISTORY tracks replication data transfer history, not overall account storage consumption.

questions.stem: An administrator notices high serverless credit charges and suspects serverless tasks (not Snowpipe). Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view provides credit consumption details per serverless task execution?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.TASK_HISTORY
options.explanation: is incorrect. TASK_HISTORY tracks task execution state and errors for all tasks (including user-managed warehouse tasks), but focuses on execution status rather than credit attribution. It does not have a per-execution credit column.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.SERVERLESS_TASK_HISTORY
options.explanation: is correct. SERVERLESS_TASK_HISTORY is the dedicated ACCOUNT_USAGE view for serverless task credit consumption. It provides per-execution records including task name, database, schema, start/end times, and credits consumed — enabling identification of which specific serverless tasks are driving credit usage.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_DAILY_HISTORY WHERE SERVICE_TYPE = 'SERVERLESS_TASK'
options.explanation: is incorrect. METERING_DAILY_HISTORY can show aggregate serverless task credits by day but does not break down by individual task name. It provides daily totals, not per-task breakdown.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.PIPE_USAGE_HISTORY
options.explanation: is incorrect. PIPE_USAGE_HISTORY is specifically for Snowpipe (continuous data ingestion), not for serverless task compute.

questions.stem: A team wants to track Snowpipe credit consumption by individual pipe to identify which data ingestion pipeline is most expensive. Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view provides this per-pipe breakdown?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_DAILY_HISTORY WHERE SERVICE_TYPE = 'SNOWPIPE'
options.explanation: is incorrect. METERING_DAILY_HISTORY shows aggregate Snowpipe credits per day but does not break down by individual pipe name. It cannot identify which specific pipe is most expensive.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.COPY_HISTORY
options.explanation: is correct. PIPE_USAGE_HISTORY provides per-pipe credit consumption records for Snowpipe. Each row includes the pipe name, database, schema, credits used, bytes inserted, and the time period — enabling cost attribution to specific ingestion pipelines.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY WHERE WAREHOUSE_TYPE = 'SNOWPIPE'
options.explanation: is incorrect. COPY_HISTORY tracks file-level load status for COPY INTO operations. It shows rows loaded and errors but does not provide credit attribution by pipe.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.PIPE_USAGE_HISTORY
options.explanation: is incorrect. WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY tracks credit consumption by virtual warehouse, not by Snowpipe. Snowpipe runs on serverless compute, not virtual warehouses, so it does not appear in WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY.

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator needs to give REPORTING_ROLE access to all tables currently in the ANALYTICS schema AND ensure access to tables created in the future. Select TWO commands that together accomplish this. (Select TWO)

options.text: GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA analytics TO ROLE reporting_role;
options.explanation: is correct. GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA analytics TO ROLE reporting_role covers all tables that exist at the time of execution. This is required to give access to existing tables.

options.text: GRANT SELECT ON FUTURE TABLES IN SCHEMA analytics TO ROLE reporting_role;
options.explanation: is correct. GRANT SELECT ON FUTURE TABLES IN SCHEMA analytics TO ROLE reporting_role ensures that all tables created after this grant is issued also automatically receive SELECT for the role. Both commands together cover existing and future tables.

options.text: GRANT SELECT ON ALL CURRENT AND FUTURE TABLES IN SCHEMA analytics TO ROLE reporting_role;
options.explanation: is incorrect. GRANT SELECT ON ALL CURRENT AND FUTURE TABLES is not valid Snowflake syntax. Snowflake requires two separate GRANT commands to cover existing and future objects.

options.text: ALTER SCHEMA analytics SET AUTO_GRANT SELECT TO ROLE reporting_role;
options.explanation: is incorrect. ALTER SCHEMA ... SET AUTO_GRANT SELECT is not valid Snowflake syntax. No AUTO_GRANT parameter exists on schemas.

questions.stem: A role ANALYST_ROLE was accidentally granted MODIFY on table SALES.ORDERS. The administrator needs to remove only the MODIFY privilege without affecting the role's SELECT privilege on the same table. Which command achieves this?

options.text: REVOKE ALL ON TABLE sales.orders FROM ROLE analyst_role;
options.explanation: is incorrect. REVOKE ALL ON TABLE sales.orders FROM ROLE analyst_role removes all privileges the role has on that table, including the desired SELECT privilege. This is too broad.

options.text: REVOKE MODIFY ON TABLE sales.orders FROM ROLE analyst_role;
options.explanation: is correct. REVOKE MODIFY ON TABLE sales.orders FROM ROLE analyst_role removes exactly the MODIFY privilege from the role on that specific table, leaving SELECT and all other privileges intact. Snowflake REVOKE is granular — it removes only the specified privilege.

options.text: ALTER ROLE analyst_role REMOVE PRIVILEGE MODIFY ON TABLE sales.orders;
options.explanation: is incorrect. ALTER ROLE ... REMOVE PRIVILEGE is not valid Snowflake syntax. Privilege revocations use the REVOKE command, not ALTER ROLE.

options.text: GRANT OWNERSHIP ON TABLE sales.orders TO ROLE sysadmin REVOKE CURRENT GRANTS;
options.explanation: is incorrect. GRANT OWNERSHIP ... REVOKE CURRENT GRANTS transfers table ownership and revokes existing grants. This is far more disruptive than revoking a single privilege and would remove access for all roles.

questions.stem: A stored procedure runs with EXECUTE AS OWNER privileges. The procedure calls IS_GRANTED_TO_INVOKER_ROLE('ADMIN_ROLE') to check if the calling user has admin privileges before executing a sensitive operation. The calling user has ADMIN_ROLE in their role hierarchy but is currently using DATA_ROLE as the active role. What does IS_GRANTED_TO_INVOKER_ROLE return?

options.text: TRUE, because ADMIN_ROLE is granted to the invoking user even though it is not their active role
options.explanation: is incorrect. While ADMIN_ROLE is granted to the user, saying TRUE because it is granted mischaracterizes the distinction. The function checks the full hierarchy, which is correct behavior — but option C states this more accurately.

options.text: FALSE, because IS_GRANTED_TO_INVOKER_ROLE only checks the currently active primary role, not the full hierarchy
options.explanation: is incorrect. IS_GRANTED_TO_INVOKER_ROLE does NOT only check the currently active primary role. It checks the full granted role hierarchy of the invoker. This is a key distinction from CURRENT_ROLE().

options.text: TRUE, because the function checks whether the invoker has ADMIN_ROLE anywhere in their granted role hierarchy
options.explanation: is correct. IS_GRANTED_TO_INVOKER_ROLE('role_name') checks whether the role is anywhere in the invoker's granted role hierarchy — not just the currently active primary role. Since the user has ADMIN_ROLE granted (even though DATA_ROLE is active), the function returns TRUE. This is the designed behavior for EXECUTE AS OWNER procedures to make invoker-context access decisions.

options.text: Error — IS_GRANTED_TO_INVOKER_ROLE cannot be called from within an EXECUTE AS OWNER procedure
options.explanation: is incorrect. IS_GRANTED_TO_INVOKER_ROLE is specifically designed to be called from within EXECUTE AS OWNER stored procedures. This is its primary documented use case.

questions.stem: A security auditor notes that a non-admin role has OWNERSHIP on several production tables. Select TWO security implications of this situation. (Select TWO)

options.text: The role can GRANT any privilege on those tables to any other role, including to PUBLIC
options.explanation: is correct. OWNERSHIP privilege grants full control over the object, including the ability to GRANT any privilege on those tables to any role — including PUBLIC. This creates a significant data exposure risk if a non-admin role owns production tables.

options.text: The role can drop those tables even if it does not have USAGE on the schema
options.explanation: is incorrect. USAGE on the schema is still required to access or drop objects within it, even for the owning role. Owning a table does not bypass schema USAGE requirements.

options.text: The role can transfer OWNERSHIP to another role, including granting it to ACCOUNTADMIN
options.explanation: is correct. The owning role can transfer OWNERSHIP to another role via GRANT OWNERSHIP. The current owner chooses which role receives the object, which could include granting it to ACCOUNTADMIN or any other role.

options.text: The role automatically inherits ACCOUNTADMIN privileges because it owns production tables
options.explanation: is incorrect. Owning objects in Snowflake does not confer any account-level privileges. ACCOUNTADMIN is a system role that must be explicitly granted — object ownership has no bearing on it.

questions.stem: A Snowflake account is consuming data from a data share provided by a vendor. The administrator needs to grant the DATA_TEAM_ROLE access to query tables in the shared database VENDOR_DB. Which privilege grant is specific to shared databases?

options.text: GRANT USAGE ON DATABASE vendor_db TO ROLE data_team_role;
options.explanation: is incorrect. GRANT USAGE ON DATABASE works for local databases but does not provide access to the contents of a shared database. Shared databases require IMPORTED PRIVILEGES.

options.text: GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN DATABASE vendor_db TO ROLE data_team_role;
options.explanation: is incorrect. GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES can be applied to local databases but cannot be applied directly to shared databases. Access to shared database objects is controlled by IMPORTED PRIVILEGES.

options.text: GRANT IMPORTED PRIVILEGES ON DATABASE vendor_db TO ROLE data_team_role;
options.explanation: is correct. For databases created from inbound data shares, GRANT IMPORTED PRIVILEGES ON DATABASE is the correct and specific privilege grant. This single grant gives the role access to all objects in the shared database as defined by the share provider.

options.text: GRANT REFERENCE_USAGE ON DATABASE vendor_db TO ROLE data_team_role;
options.explanation: is incorrect. REFERENCE_USAGE is a different concept used for cross-database view references when creating views that reference objects in other databases. It is not used for granting access to inbound shared databases.

questions.stem: An administrator needs to view all objects (tables, schemas, warehouses) that REPORTING_ROLE currently has privileges on, and who originally granted those privileges. Which command provides this?

options.text: SHOW GRANTS TO ROLE reporting_role;
options.explanation: is correct. SHOW GRANTS TO ROLE reporting_role lists all privileges granted to that role, including the privilege type, object type, object name, and the GRANTED_BY column showing who issued each grant. This is the command for viewing what a role can do and who granted those rights.

options.text: SHOW GRANTS OF ROLE reporting_role;
options.explanation: is incorrect. SHOW GRANTS OF ROLE reporting_role shows which users and roles have been granted this role — i.e., who holds REPORTING_ROLE in their hierarchy. It does not show what privileges the role itself has.

options.text: DESCRIBE ROLE reporting_role;
options.explanation: is incorrect. DESCRIBE ROLE shows basic role metadata (name, comment, owner) but does not list the role's granted privileges.

options.text: SHOW ROLE reporting_role GRANTS;
options.explanation: is incorrect. SHOW ROLE reporting_role GRANTS is not valid Snowflake syntax. The correct commands are SHOW GRANTS TO ROLE or SHOW GRANTS OF ROLE.

questions.stem: A company deploys a Snowflake Native App. The app defines two Application Roles: APP_VIEWER and APP_ADMIN. A consumer account administrator wants to give their ANALYST_ROLE users access to the app's viewer functionality. What is the correct step?

options.text: GRANT APPLICATION ROLE myapp.app_viewer TO ROLE analyst_role;
options.explanation: is correct. To grant a Native App's Application Role to an account role, the syntax is GRANT APPLICATION ROLE app_name.role_name TO ROLE account_role. In this case: GRANT APPLICATION ROLE myapp.app_viewer TO ROLE analyst_role assigns the app's viewer Application Role to the consumer's ANALYST_ROLE.

options.text: GRANT USAGE ON APPLICATION myapp TO ROLE analyst_role;
options.explanation: is incorrect. GRANT USAGE ON APPLICATION grants access to the application itself but does not assign a specific Application Role within it. Application Roles must be granted explicitly.

options.text: ALTER APPLICATION myapp GRANT VIEWER TO ROLE analyst_role;
options.explanation: is incorrect. ALTER APPLICATION myapp GRANT VIEWER is not valid Snowflake syntax. Native App role assignment uses GRANT APPLICATION ROLE.

options.text: GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN APPLICATION myapp TO ROLE analyst_role;
options.explanation: is incorrect. GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN APPLICATION is not valid Snowflake syntax for Native Apps. Access to Native App content is managed through Application Roles, not direct object privilege grants.

questions.reference: [About the Snowflake Native App Framework | Snowflake Documentation](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/native-apps/native-apps-about)

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator has applied an account-level network policy. Later, a specific service account SVC_INTEGRATION needs to connect from an IP not in the account policy. The administrator creates a new policy SVC_POLICY that includes the service account's IP. Which command applies this policy specifically to the service account?

options.text: ALTER USER svc_integration SET NETWORK_POLICY = svc_policy;
options.explanation: is correct. Network policies are applied to individual users using ALTER USER user_name SET NETWORK_POLICY = policy_name. A user-level network policy overrides the account-level policy for that specific user, making it the mechanism for per-user network policy exceptions.

options.text: ALTER ACCOUNT OVERRIDE NETWORK_POLICY = svc_policy FOR USER svc_integration;
options.explanation: is incorrect. ALTER ACCOUNT OVERRIDE NETWORK_POLICY FOR USER is not valid Snowflake syntax. Per-user network policies are set via ALTER USER.

options.text: GRANT NETWORK POLICY svc_policy TO USER svc_integration;
options.explanation: is incorrect. GRANT NETWORK POLICY is not valid Snowflake syntax. Network policies are applied via ALTER statements, not granted.

options.text: CREATE NETWORK POLICY EXCEPTION FOR USER svc_integration USING svc_policy;
options.explanation: is incorrect. CREATE NETWORK POLICY EXCEPTION FOR USER is not valid Snowflake syntax. No exception construct exists — per-user policies are handled by assigning a separate policy to the user.

questions.reference: [Controlling Network Traffic with Network Policies | Snowflake Documentation](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/network-policies)

questions.stem: An administrator wants to find all network policies defined in the Snowflake account and see their allowed and blocked IP lists. Which command provides this?

options.text: SHOW NETWORK POLICIES;
options.explanation: is correct. SHOW NETWORK POLICIES lists all network policies in the account with their name, comment, entry count for allowed and blocked lists, and assignment details. Full IP lists can be viewed with DESCRIBE NETWORK POLICY policy_name.

options.text: SELECT * FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.NETWORK_POLICIES;
options.explanation: is incorrect. There is no SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.NETWORK_POLICIES view. Network policy information is obtained via SHOW NETWORK POLICIES and DESCRIBE NETWORK POLICY.

options.text: LIST NETWORK POLICIES;
options.explanation: is incorrect. LIST NETWORK POLICIES is not valid Snowflake syntax. LIST is used for listing files on stages, not for network policies.

options.text: DESCRIBE NETWORK POLICIES ALL;
options.explanation: is incorrect. DESCRIBE NETWORK POLICIES ALL is not valid Snowflake syntax. Network policies are described individually using DESCRIBE NETWORK POLICY policy_name.
