questions.reference: [Controlling Network Traffic with Network Policies | Snowflake Documentation](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/network-policies)

questions.stem: A Snowflake external stage pointing to an Azure Blob container is failing with an access error. An administrator suspects a misconfigured storage integration. Which Snowflake function can verify whether the stage can successfully connect to the external location?

options.text: SYSTEM$VERIFY_EXTERNAL_STAGE_CONNECTION('@my_azure_stage')
options.explanation: is correct. SYSTEM$VERIFY_EXTERNAL_STAGE_CONNECTION('@stage_name') validates the connection to an external stage by testing whether Snowflake can authenticate and access the cloud storage location. It returns diagnostic information about connectivity and permission state, helping diagnose misconfigured storage integrations.

options.text: SYSTEM$CHECK_STORAGE_INTEGRATION('my_azure_integration')
options.explanation: is incorrect. SYSTEM$CHECK_STORAGE_INTEGRATION('integration_name') is not a valid Snowflake system function. No such function exists.

options.text: SYSTEM$TEST_STAGE_CONNECTIVITY('@my_azure_stage')
options.explanation: is incorrect. SYSTEM$TEST_STAGE_CONNECTIVITY('@stage_name') is not a valid Snowflake system function. The correct function is SYSTEM$VERIFY_EXTERNAL_STAGE_CONNECTION.

options.text: SELECT STAGE_STATUS FROM INFORMATION_SCHEMA.STAGES WHERE STAGE_NAME = 'MY_AZURE_STAGE';
options.explanation: is incorrect. INFORMATION_SCHEMA.STAGES does not have a real-time STAGE_STATUS connectivity column. It contains stage definition metadata, not live connectivity status.

questions.stem: An organization uses Snowflake on AWS with PrivateLink enabled. They have a network policy with ALLOWED_IP_LIST = ('203.0.113.0/24'). A client connecting through PrivateLink from a VPC with private IP 10.0.1.5 (not in the allowed list) attempts to connect. What occurs?

options.text: The connection is blocked because 10.0.1.5 is not in the ALLOWED_IP_LIST
options.explanation: is incorrect. The connection does not fail based on the private IP not being in the ALLOWED_IP_LIST because IP-based network policies do not apply to PrivateLink traffic. PrivateLink bypasses IP-based network policy evaluation.

options.text: The connection succeeds because PrivateLink traffic bypasses IP-based network policy enforcement
options.explanation: is correct. When clients connect through AWS PrivateLink, their traffic arrives at Snowflake without presenting a public IP address that IP-based network policies can evaluate. Standard IP-based network policies are bypassed for PrivateLink connections. To enforce restrictions for PrivateLink traffic, use AWSVPCEID-type network rules to restrict by VPC endpoint ID.

options.text: The connection is blocked because PrivateLink requires a separate AWSVPCEID network rule, not an IP policy
options.explanation: is incorrect. While using AWSVPCEID network rules is the recommended approach for PrivateLink access control, the absence of such a rule does not block the connection. Without AWSVPCEID rules, PrivateLink connections succeed regardless of IP policies.

options.text: The connection is blocked and an alert is sent to the NOTIFY_USERS list
options.explanation: is incorrect. Blocked connections do not automatically trigger resource monitor notification alerts. NOTIFY_USERS on resource monitors is for credit threshold alerts, not network policy blocks.

questions.reference: [Controlling Network Traffic with Network Policies | Snowflake Documentation](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/network-policies)

questions.stem: A security team wants to restrict Snowflake access to only connections originating from a specific set of approved AWS VPC endpoints (vpce-0a1b2c3d, vpce-0e5f6789). Which is the correct CREATE NETWORK RULE command for this?

options.text: CREATE NETWORK RULE vpc_rule TYPE = AWSVPCEID VALUE_LIST = ('vpce-0a1b2c3d', 'vpce-0e5f6789') MODE = INGRESS;
options.explanation: is correct. The correct syntax to restrict access to specific VPC endpoints uses TYPE = AWSVPCEID and lists the endpoint IDs in VALUE_LIST. MODE = INGRESS specifies inbound connection control. This network rule is then referenced within a network policy for enforcement.

options.text: CREATE NETWORK POLICY vpc_policy ALLOWED_VPC_LIST = ('vpce-0a1b2c3d', 'vpce-0e5f6789');
options.explanation: is incorrect. ALLOWED_VPC_LIST is not a valid network policy parameter. Network policies use ALLOWED_NETWORK_RULE_LIST and BLOCKED_NETWORK_RULE_LIST to reference network rules.

options.text: CREATE NETWORK RULE vpc_rule TYPE = IPV4 VALUE_LIST = ('vpce-0a1b2c3d', 'vpce-0e5f6789');
options.explanation: is incorrect. TYPE = IPV4 with VPC endpoint IDs is incorrect — VPC endpoint IDs (vpce-xxxxx) are not IP addresses and cannot be used with the IPV4 type. The correct type is AWSVPCEID.

options.text: ALTER ACCOUNT SET VPC_ALLOWED_LIST = ('vpce-0a1b2c3d', 'vpce-0e5f6789');
options.explanation: is incorrect. ALTER ACCOUNT SET VPC_ALLOWED_LIST is not a valid Snowflake account parameter. VPC endpoint restrictions are configured through network rules and network policies.

questions.stem: An administrator notices that a staging table used for ETL has TIME_TRAVEL_BYTES = 45GB but the table only holds 2GB of active data. The staging table is overwritten every night. What is the most effective way to eliminate this wasted Time Travel storage?

options.text: Reduce DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS at the account level to 0
options.explanation: is incorrect. Reducing retention at the account level to 0 would affect all tables account-wide, disabling Time Travel for tables where it is needed. This violates the principle of least impact.

options.text: Run TRUNCATE TABLE staging_table to clear the Time Travel history
options.explanation: is incorrect. TRUNCATE TABLE clears current data but does not immediately eliminate existing Time Travel history. The historical micro-partitions persist until the retention period expires.

options.text: ALTER TABLE staging_table SET DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 0;
options.explanation: is correct. ALTER TABLE staging_table SET DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 0 disables Time Travel for that specific table, eliminating the TIME_TRAVEL_BYTES storage cost. This is the precise, targeted fix that does not affect other tables.

options.text: Enable automatic clustering on the staging table to compact and merge old micro-partitions
options.explanation: is incorrect. Automatic clustering reorganizes micro-partitions for query performance. It does not reduce Time Travel storage and would actually increase costs if enabled on a staging table.

questions.stem: A developer creates both a TEMPORARY table and a TRANSIENT table with the same schema in a Snowflake session. What happens to the TEMPORARY table when the session ends?

options.text: The temporary table is automatically dropped at session end; the transient table persists
options.explanation: is correct. Temporary tables are session-scoped — they are automatically dropped when the creating session ends and are not visible to other sessions. Transient tables, like permanent tables, persist across sessions and are visible account-wide. They differ from permanent tables only in having no Fail-Safe period and limited Time Travel retention (0 or 1 day).

options.text: Both tables are dropped when the session ends because both types are session-scoped
options.explanation: is incorrect. Only temporary tables are dropped at session end. Transient tables persist after the session ends and remain visible to other sessions.

options.text: Both tables persist across sessions — the difference is only in their Fail-Safe behavior
options.explanation: is incorrect. Temporary tables do not persist across sessions. They are automatically dropped at session end. The difference between transient and permanent is Fail-Safe and extended Time Travel, not session scope.

options.text: The temporary table persists for 24 hours after session end before automatic deletion
options.explanation: is incorrect. Temporary tables are not retained for 24 hours after session end. They are dropped immediately when the session terminates.

questions.stem: An administrator wants to verify the DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS setting for all tables in a specific schema to identify tables with non-standard retention values. Which approach correctly retrieves this information?

options.text: SHOW TABLES IN SCHEMA mydb.myschema; — check the retention_time column
options.explanation: is correct. SHOW TABLES IN SCHEMA schema_name outputs a row per table with a retention_time column showing the effective DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS for each table. This is the quickest way to review retention settings across a schema.

options.text: DESCRIBE SCHEMA mydb.myschema TABLES;
options.explanation: is incorrect. DESCRIBE SCHEMA ... TABLES is not valid Snowflake syntax. DESCRIBE works on individual objects, not schema-level table listings.

options.text: SELECT TABLE_NAME, DATA_RETENTION_DAYS FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.TABLES WHERE TABLE_SCHEMA = 'MYSCHEMA';
options.explanation: is partially correct in concept — ACCOUNT_USAGE.TABLES does have a RETENTION_TIME column — but the column name in the query is wrong (DATA_RETENTION_DAYS vs RETENTION_TIME). SHOW TABLES is simpler and more direct for this use case.

options.text: SHOW DATABASES LIKE 'MYDB' TABLES;
options.explanation: is incorrect. SHOW DATABASES LIKE 'MYDB' TABLES is not valid Snowflake syntax. SHOW DATABASES filters databases by name pattern; it does not support a TABLES modifier.

questions.stem: A Snowflake warehouse is currently of type STANDARD. A Snowpark team needs a warehouse with high memory for ML training. Which command changes the warehouse type to SNOWPARK-OPTIMIZED?

options.text: ALTER WAREHOUSE ml_wh SET WAREHOUSE_TYPE = SNOWPARK-OPTIMIZED;
options.explanation: is correct. ALTER WAREHOUSE warehouse_name SET WAREHOUSE_TYPE = SNOWPARK-OPTIMIZED is the correct syntax to change an existing warehouse to the Snowpark-optimized type. This change can be applied to a running or suspended warehouse and takes effect on the next warehouse start.

options.text: DROP WAREHOUSE ml_wh; CREATE WAREHOUSE ml_wh TYPE = SNOWPARK-OPTIMIZED;
options.explanation: is incorrect. While dropping and recreating the warehouse also works, it destroys any warehouse-level resource monitor assignments, query history, and requires reassigning any grants — unnecessary when ALTER WAREHOUSE supports the type change directly.

options.text: ALTER WAREHOUSE ml_wh SET MEMORY_OPTIMIZED = TRUE;
options.explanation: is incorrect. MEMORY_OPTIMIZED = TRUE is not a valid warehouse parameter. There is no such parameter in Snowflake.

options.text: ALTER WAREHOUSE ml_wh MODIFY TYPE = 'SNOWPARK_OPTIMIZED';
options.explanation: is incorrect. ALTER WAREHOUSE MODIFY TYPE = 'SNOWPARK_OPTIMIZED' is not valid Snowflake syntax. The correct form uses SET WAREHOUSE_TYPE = SNOWPARK-OPTIMIZED (with a hyphen, not an underscore).

questions.stem: An administrator queries TABLE_STORAGE_METRICS and finds TABLE_A has ACTIVE_BYTES = 10GB, TIME_TRAVEL_BYTES = 50GB, FAILSAFE_BYTES = 40GB. Select TWO accurate statements about this storage profile. (Select TWO)

options.text: Setting DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 0 on TABLE_A immediately drops the 50GB of TIME_TRAVEL_BYTES
options.explanation: is incorrect. Setting DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 0 stops new Time Travel data from accumulating, but it does not immediately purge existing TIME_TRAVEL_BYTES. Historical micro-partitions age out via background processes over time.

options.text: The 40GB of FAILSAFE_BYTES are billable to the customer but are only recoverable by Snowflake Support
options.explanation: is correct. Fail-Safe storage (FAILSAFE_BYTES) is billed to the customer at the same rate as active storage, but it is only accessible by Snowflake Support. Customers cannot query, restore, or access Fail-Safe data directly — only Snowflake Support can initiate a Fail-Safe recovery.

options.text: The high TIME_TRAVEL_BYTES relative to ACTIVE_BYTES suggests the table has a high rate of DML changes (updates/deletes) creating many historical versions
options.explanation: is correct. A high ratio of TIME_TRAVEL_BYTES (50GB) to ACTIVE_BYTES (10GB) typically indicates a high volume of DML operations (updates, deletes) that created many historical micro-partition versions. Each DML operation creates new micro-partitions while the old ones are retained in Time Travel.

options.text: Cloning TABLE_A to create TABLE_A_CLONE will double the ACTIVE_BYTES cost immediately
options.explanation: is incorrect. Zero-copy cloning does not duplicate ACTIVE_BYTES immediately. Clones share the underlying micro-partitions with the source table; only data that diverges (through DML on either the source or clone) creates additional storage.

questions.stem: A security team needs to audit all data that was transferred out of the Snowflake account to external locations (external stages, S3 buckets, etc.) over the past 60 days. Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view provides this egress data transfer history?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.COPY_HISTORY
options.explanation: is incorrect. COPY_HISTORY tracks file-level load status for COPY INTO <table> (ingest) operations. It focuses on rows loaded and errors, not on data egress auditing or transfer volumes.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.STAGE_USAGE_HISTORY
options.explanation: is correct. ACCOUNT_USAGE.DATA_TRANSFER_HISTORY records all data transfers between Snowflake and external locations, including COPY INTO <external_stage> (unload) operations. It includes transfer direction, bytes transferred, source/destination regions, and timestamps — making it the primary view for egress auditing.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.ACCESS_HISTORY WHERE QUERY_TYPE = 'UNLOAD'
options.explanation: is incorrect. STAGE_USAGE_HISTORY is not a standard SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE view. No such view exists in Snowflake's Account Usage schema.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.DATA_TRANSFER_HISTORY
options.explanation: is incorrect. ACCESS_HISTORY tracks which data objects were accessed during query execution (read/write lineage). Filtering by query type does not provide egress transfer auditing; DATA_TRANSFER_HISTORY is the designated view for this.

questions.stem: A compliance team needs to identify all client applications connecting to Snowflake and flag any outdated JDBC driver versions. Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view and column combination provides this client application tracking?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.LOGIN_HISTORY — CLIENT_APPLICATION_ID column
options.explanation: is incorrect. LOGIN_HISTORY has CLIENT_APPLICATION_ID per login event, but captures only authentication events — not the full session context or per-session client environment details.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.SESSIONS — CLIENT_APPLICATION_ID and CLIENT_ENVIRONMENT columns
options.explanation: is correct. ACCOUNT_USAGE.SESSIONS provides the most comprehensive client application tracking. CLIENT_APPLICATION_ID contains the application name and version (e.g., 'JDBC 3.13.22'), and CLIENT_ENVIRONMENT contains additional platform metadata. Querying SESSIONS enables identification of all distinct client applications and filtering for outdated driver versions.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY — CLIENT_APPLICATION_ID column
options.explanation: is incorrect. QUERY_HISTORY has CLIENT_APPLICATION_ID per query but requires deduplication across many rows to identify unique clients. SESSIONS is more directly structured for this use case.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.USERS — CLIENT_VERSION column
options.explanation: is incorrect. ACCOUNT_USAGE.USERS does not have a CLIENT_VERSION column. USER records capture account-level user configuration, not runtime client connection details.

questions.stem: An ORGADMIN user wants to view credit usage across all accounts in the Snowflake organization to identify which accounts are highest consumers. Which schema and view provides this cross-account visibility?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_DAILY_HISTORY — queried from any account in the org
options.explanation: is incorrect. ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_DAILY_HISTORY is scoped to a single account and cannot be queried to see credit usage across multiple accounts in an organization.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ORG_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY — available to all accounts in the organization
options.explanation: is correct. Organization-level credit visibility is available through the ORGANIZATION_USAGE schema, accessible to users with the ORGADMIN role from the organization administrator account. Views like USAGE_IN_CURRENCY_DAILY and METERING_DAILY_HISTORY in ORGANIZATION_USAGE provide cross-account credit consumption data.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_DAILY_HISTORY — with a cross-account JOIN using ACCOUNT_LOCATOR
options.explanation: is incorrect. ORG_USAGE is not the correct schema name. The correct schema for organization-level data is ORGANIZATION_USAGE, not ORG_USAGE.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ORGANIZATION_USAGE.USAGE_IN_CURRENCY_DAILY — accessed by ORGADMIN from the org admin account
options.explanation: is incorrect. ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_DAILY_HISTORY cannot be joined across accounts — each account has its own isolated ACCOUNT_USAGE schema. There is no cross-account JOIN mechanism available to regular users.
